From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Former school of Yale University

Sheffield Scientific School

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Chemistry Class in 1898 | |
| Type | [Private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") |
| Active | 1847 (1847)–1956 (1956) |
| Affiliation | [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") |
| Location | [New Haven](/wiki/New_Haven "New Haven") , [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") , United States |

**Sheffield Scientific School** was founded in 1847 as a school of [Yale College](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") in [New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/New_Haven,_Connecticut "New Haven, Connecticut"), for instruction in science and engineering. Originally named the **Yale Scientific School**, it was renamed in 1861 in honor of [Joseph E. Sheffield](/wiki/Joseph_E._Sheffield "Joseph E. Sheffield"), a railroad executive. The school was incorporated in 1871. The Sheffield Scientific School helped establish a model of American higher education which incorporated both the sciences and the liberal arts. Following [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), its curriculum gradually became completely integrated with Yale College. "**The Sheff**" ceased to function as a separate entity in 1956.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Scientific_School&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Yale_President%27s_House.jpg/250px-Yale_President%27s_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yale_President%27s_House.jpg)

Second President's House, home to the Department of Philosophy and the Arts, 1847–1860

After technological developments in the early nineteenth century, such as the [electric telegraph](/wiki/Electric_telegraph "Electric telegraph"), an interest was fostered in teaching applied science at universities. [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") established the [Lawrence Scientific School](/wiki/Lawrence_Scientific_School "Lawrence Scientific School") in 1846 and [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") began the [Chandler Scientific School](/wiki/Chandler_Scientific_School "Chandler Scientific School") in 1852. The stage was set at Yale for the transition in education beginning in 1846, when professorships of *agricultural chemistry* ([John Pitkin Norton](/wiki/John_Pitkin_Norton "John Pitkin Norton")) and *practical chemistry* ([Benjamin Silliman Jr.](/wiki/Benjamin_Silliman_Jr. "Benjamin Silliman Jr.")) were established. In 1847, the School of Applied Chemistry became part of a newly created *Department of Philosophy and the Arts* (later, the [Yale Graduate School](/wiki/Yale_Graduate_School "Yale Graduate School")). Classes and labs were hosted in the Second President's House on Yale's [Old Campus](/wiki/Old_Campus "Old Campus") until funding and a suitable facility could be found.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Norton died in 1852 and was followed by [John Addison Porter](/wiki/John_Addison_Porter "John Addison Porter"). The professorship for applied chemistry was followed in 1852 by one for *civil engineering,* ([William Augustus Norton](/wiki/William_Augustus_Norton "William Augustus Norton")) establishing a school of engineering. These programs made up the Yale Scientific School.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Joseph_Earl_Sheffield.jpg/250px-Joseph_Earl_Sheffield.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joseph_Earl_Sheffield.jpg)

Joseph Earl Sheffield (1793-1882), the school's namesake

In 1853 and 1854, science and engineering courses were listed in the Yale College course catalog as offered by the Yale Scientific School. Porter elicited help from his father-in-law, [Joseph Earl Sheffield](/wiki/Joseph_Earl_Sheffield "Joseph Earl Sheffield") (1793-1882), and in 1858, Sheffield donated over US$100,000 to purchase the old Medical Department building for the scientific school. This gift included two newly-renovated wings within the building.[[2]](#cite_note-last-dino-hunter-2): 8 The old [Yale Medical School](/wiki/Yale_Medical_School "Yale Medical School") building on the northeast corner of Grove and Prospect Streets was renovated and renamed (South) Sheffield Hall. (It was demolished in 1931 and was on the current site of *Sterling Tower, Sheffield Hall and Strathcona Hall* (SSS).) Sheffield's building reinforced the division of [Hillhouse Avenue](/wiki/Hillhouse_Avenue "Hillhouse Avenue") into an upper, residential section, and a lower section devoted to education. In 1861, the school became the Sheffield Scientific School.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sheffield was one of Yale's greatest benefactors and continued to support the school throughout his life, giving a total of about US$500,000. Yale also received US$591,000 from his will as well as his house, the Sheffield mansion, designed and originally owned by [Ithiel Town](/wiki/Ithiel_Town "Ithiel Town") (demolished in 1957).[[3]](#cite_note-havemeyer-3) The school also benefited from the [Morrill Act](/wiki/Morrill_Act "Morrill Act") starting in 1863 and an agricultural course was begun. Land grant status, however, was transferred to the [Storrs Agricultural School](/wiki/University_of_Connecticut "University of Connecticut") in 1893 after arguments by the state [grange](/wiki/Grange_movement "Grange movement") that the school was not a proper "farm school".[[4]](#cite_note-uconn-4)

## Education and student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Scientific_School&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Education and student life")]

The Sheffield School innovated with an undergraduate course offering science and mathematics as well as economics, English, geography, history, modern languages, philology and political science. Sheffield also pioneered graduate education in the United States, granting the first Ph.D. in the United States in 1861 as well as the first engineering Ph.D. to [Josiah Willard Gibbs](/wiki/Josiah_Willard_Gibbs "Josiah Willard Gibbs") in 1863, and the first geology Ph.D. to [William North Rice](/wiki/William_North_Rice "William North Rice") in 1867.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Unlike Yale College students at the time, Sheffield students had "no dorms, no required chapel, no disciplinary marks and no proctors".[[5]](#cite_note-kelly-5) The Academical Department of Yale (*Ac*) and Sheffield (*Sheff*) became rivals. [Loomis Havemeyer](/wiki/Loomis_Havemeyer "Loomis Havemeyer"), alumnus and registrar at Sheffield, stated: "During the second half of the nineteenth century Yale College and Sheffield Scientific School, separated by only a few streets, were two separate countries on the same planet." The *Ac* students studied liberal arts and would look down on the practical *Sheff* students.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sheffield had its own student [secret societies](/wiki/Secret_societies "Secret societies"), including the Colony Club, 1848 (now [Berzelius](/wiki/Berzelius_(secret_society) "Berzelius (secret society)")), the Cloister, 1863 (now [Book and Snake](/wiki/Book_and_Snake "Book and Snake")), [St. Anthony Hall](/wiki/St._Anthony_Hall "St. Anthony Hall"), 1867 (now a 3-year society, also called Delta Psi), [St. Elmo](/wiki/St._Elmo_(secret_society) "St. Elmo (secret society)"), 1889 (also a senior society), as well as Franklin Hall, 1865 ([Theta Xi](/wiki/Theta_Xi "Theta Xi")), York Hall, 1877 (Chi Phi), Sachem Hall, 1893 ([Phi Sigma Kappa](/wiki/Phi_Sigma_Kappa "Phi Sigma Kappa")), and Vernon Hall, 1908 (now [Myth and Sword](/wiki/Myth_and_Sword "Myth and Sword")). The *[Yale Scientific](/wiki/Yale_Scientific "Yale Scientific")* magazine was founded at Sheffield in 1894, the first student magazine devoted to the sciences.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Other buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Scientific_School&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other buildings")]

In 1872–73, Sheffield Scientific School's first new building, North Sheffield Hall was built, designed by [Josiah Cleaveland Cady](/wiki/J._Cleaveland_Cady "J. Cleaveland Cady"), on what had been the gardens of the Town-Sheffield mansion. This was followed by Winchester Hall (1892) and Sheffield Chemical (1894-5, J. Cleaveland Cady). Of these, only the latter, Sheffield Chemical, is still standing, renovated and renamed [Arthur K. Watson](/wiki/Arthur_K._Watson "Arthur K. Watson") Hall. Becton Laboratory (designed by [Marcel Breuer](/wiki/Marcel_Breuer "Marcel Breuer"), 1970) now stands on the site of North Sheffield and Winchester Halls (demolished in 1967). Further expansion brought Kirtland Hall (1902, [Kirtland Cutter](/wiki/Kirtland_Cutter "Kirtland Cutter")), Hammond Laboratory (1904, W. Gedney Beatty), Leet Oliver Hall (1908, [Charles C. Haight](/wiki/Charles_C._Haight "Charles C. Haight")), Mason Laboratory (1911, Charles C. Haight) and Dunham Laboratory (1912, Henry Morse; addition 1958, Douglas Orr), all still standing except Hammond which was razed in 2009 to make way for two new residential colleges.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Reorganization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sheffield_Scientific_School&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Reorganization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Sheffield-plaque-yale-new-haven-usa.jpg/330px-Sheffield-plaque-yale-new-haven-usa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sheffield-plaque-yale-new-haven-usa.jpg)

Plaque commemorating Sheffield Hall

During the 1918-1919 reorganization of the educational structure of Yale University, the three years "select" course at Sheffield Scientific School was eliminated and a four-year course of study for those studying "professional science" and "engineering" was approved, while graduate courses were transferred to the Graduate School, leaving only undergraduate courses taught at Sheffield Scientific School from 1919 to 1945, coexisting with Yale College's science programs. The centennial was celebrated in 1947 with the [Silliman lectures](/wiki/Silliman_lecture "Silliman lecture") given by [Ernest O. Lawrence](/wiki/Ernest_O._Lawrence "Ernest O. Lawrence"), [Linus Pauling](/wiki/Linus_Pauling "Linus Pauling"), [W. M. Stanley](/wiki/W._M._Stanley "W. M. Stanley") and [George Wells Beadle](/wiki/George_Wells_Beadle "George Wells Beadle").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The first degree of [Bachelor of Science](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") was awarded in 1922 to the graduating class of the Sheffield Scientific School. In 1932, the School of Engineering was reestablished and Sheffield Scientific School engineering classes were transferred to the new school. In 1945, the Sheffield Scientific School resumed its original function of graduate level instruction in science. Undergraduate courses for the Bachelor of Science degree were transferred to Yale College, and undergraduate courses for a Bachelor of Science in industrial administration were transferred to the School of Engineering.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

This transition occurred gradually, through the influence of "aggressive, powerful alumni" (including Edwin Oviatt, editor of the *Yale Alumni Weekly*) who "took control out of [President Hadley](/wiki/Arthur_Twining_Hadley "Arthur Twining Hadley")'s hands and forced a radical reorganization of Yale".[[5]](#cite_note-kelly-5) In 1956, the Sheffield Scientific School was terminated as an active school. The Board of Trustees still exists to oversee the Sheffield Scientific School property and meet legal requirements. The school's faculty is defined as teachers of science to graduate students under the Division of Science. Engineering teaching and research is now conducted within the [School of Engineering & Applied Science](/wiki/Yale_School_of_Engineering_%26_Applied_Science "Yale School of Engineering & Applied Science").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
